title: Cauliflower cheese
subtitle: Proper cheese sauce, browned breadcrumb top
excerpt: A whole cauliflower blanketed in a thick, sharp cheddar sauce and baked until bubbling and golden on top. A Sunday side that many people eat as a main course.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Cauliflower with white sauce and cheese.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. White sauce and baked vegetable dishes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and blanch the cauliflower and make the sauce up to 24 hours ahead. Combine, cover, and refrigerate. Add an extra 10 minutes to the oven time.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white sauce made by cooking flour and butter together to form a roux, then whisking in hot milk until thick and smooth. The base of many baked dishes.
Briefly cooking vegetables in boiling water then plunging into cold water to stop cooking, preserving colour and leaving a little texture.

content.text: Cauliflower cheese is a two-part recipe: a blanched cauliflower and a sharp cheddar sauce poured over and baked until golden. The sauce needs to be well-seasoned and thick enough to cling to the florets; a thin sauce produces a watery dish that separates in the oven.
What you need
Method
Blanch the cauliflower
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan.
Blanch
the cauliflower florets in a large pan of salted boiling water for 5 minutes. Drain thoroughly and leave to steam-dry for a few minutes.
Make the cheese sauce
Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Add the flour and stir continuously for 2 minutes to make a roux. It should look like a smooth paste and smell faintly nutty.
Gradually whisk in the hot milk, a little at a time, whisking out any lumps before each addition. This is a
béchamel
base. Cook, stirring, until thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.
Remove from the heat. Stir in the 120 g of grated cheddar, the mustard, a generous grating of nutmeg, and salt to taste.
Assemble and bake
Arrange the drained cauliflower in a pie dish or large gratin dish. Pour the cheese sauce over so it covers the florets completely.
Mix the breadcrumbs and remaining grated cheddar and scatter over the top. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until bubbling and deeply golden.

items.cause: The milk was added too quickly without whisking, or the roux was too hot.
items.fix: Add the milk very gradually, whisking constantly after each addition. If lumps form, pass the sauce through a sieve.

items.symptom: The dish is watery after baking
items.cause: The cauliflower was not drained well enough, or the sauce was too thin.
items.fix: Steam-dry the blanched cauliflower for several minutes. The sauce should be thick enough to hold its shape when poured.

items.symptom: The top is pale rather than golden
items.cause: Not enough breadcrumbs and cheese, or the oven was not hot enough.
items.fix: A good layer of breadcrumbs and cheese browns well. If still pale after 30 minutes, turn the grill on for 3 minutes.

items.cause: Underseasoned, or mild cheese was used.
items.fix: Use a mature cheddar. Season generously with salt; cheese sauce needs more salt than you expect.

content.text: Variations

Smoked cheddar or a mix of cheddar and Gruyere gives a more complex flavour. Adding a tablespoon of wholegrain mustard in place of Dijon gives a coarser, more pungent sauce. Broccoli works in the same recipe, either alone or mixed with the cauliflower.
Where this dish lives

Cauliflower cheese has been a British Sunday side since the mid-nineteenth century. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton described cauliflower in white sauce as a standard accompaniment. The cheese version became dominant in post-war British cooking as cheddar became the nation's everyday cooking cheese. It is now the dish most likely to convert someone who claims not to like cauliflower.
